name: DARPA
overview: DARPA is the US Defense Department's blue-sky research arm, and AI has been one of its long-running bets — from the 2018 'AI Next' campaign through programmes on trustworthy AI (ANSR), adversarial robustness (GARD), autonomous dogfighting (ACE) and AI-driven cyber defence (AIxCC). Unusually for defence, much of its AI work is openly published: results, open-source code, and named university and industry performers. Its job is to prove hard things are possible, then hand finished capability to the operators — increasingly to the Pentagon's Chief Digital and AI Office (CDAO).

established.label: The $2B AI push
established.plain: In 2018 DARPA committed more than $2 billion across roughly 50 new and existing programmes to push AI to a 'third wave' — machines that can adapt to context, not just pattern-match. It set the agenda for most of the AI work that followed, including explainability, robustness and commonsense reasoning. Think of it as the umbrella the later programmes grew under.

established.claim: GARD — Guaranteeing AI Robustness Against Deception
established.label: Stopping enemies tricking AI
established.plain: AI vision systems can be fooled — a few stickers on a sign, or a clever pattern, can make a targeting system mistake friend for foe. GARD built defences and testing tools so military AI is harder to deceive, plus open-source kit (like the 'Armory' tester) that any AI developer can use. It ran from around 2019 and wound down in 2024.

established.claim: ANSR — Assured Neuro Symbolic Learning and Reasoning
established.label: Making AI you can trust
established.plain: Today's neural-network AI is powerful but a 'black box' you can't fully verify — a problem when lives depend on it. ANSR funds a hybrid approach that bolts logical, rule-based reasoning onto machine learning, so a system's behaviour can be checked and trusted. Launched 2022 with university and industry teams including UCLA, SRI, Carnegie Mellon and Berkeley.

established.label: First AI vs human dogfight
established.plain: ACE put AI in the pilot's seat of a real fighter jet — the X-62A, a modified F-16 — and in 2023 flew it in close-range air combat against a human-piloted F-16. It was a world first: an AI flying a real jet in a dogfight, with human safety pilots aboard who never had to take over. The point isn't replacing pilots but building trust in AI as a wingman.
established.sourceName: DARPA — ACE world first for AI in aerospace

established.label: Handing finished work to the Pentagon
established.plain: When GARD wrapped up in 2024, DARPA passed its adversarial-AI defence tools to the Chief Digital and AI Office (CDAO) — the Pentagon's hub for putting AI into wide use. This is DARPA's whole model in action: prove the hard research, then hand it to the operators rather than keeping it. Some tools were also open-sourced with Google.

inProgress.label: AI that finds and fixes bugs
inProgress.plain: A two-year competition challenging teams to build AI that automatically finds and patches security flaws in the open-source software running critical infrastructure. It finished in August 2025, but the lasting product is in progress: all seven finalist systems are being released as free open-source software so anyone can use them to harden real code.

inProgress.label: A national-security AI research forum
inProgress.plain: Announced in June 2026 with the NSF and NIST's AI standards centre, AI Forge gathers universities, industry and government to tackle AI problems that matter for security but that companies won't fund — making AI understandable (interpretability), controllable, and hard to attack. It pairs academic talent with frontier-scale compute. DARPA was gathering interested universities with a June 2026 deadline.

inProgress.label: AI fighter keeps flying
inProgress.plain: ACE didn't stop at the 2023 dogfight. Testing has continued at Edwards Air Force Base, with more than 60 AI-controlled flights logged into 2026 — including an AI autonomously evading a simulated incoming missile. The work feeds the Air Force's wider push toward AI 'collaborative combat aircraft' flying alongside crewed jets.
inProgress.sourceName: DefenseScoop — ACE AI dogfighting flight tests

latest.label: Newest AI programme
latest.plain: DARPA's most recent public AI move: a call to universities to join the AI Forge research forum, focused on AI interpretability, control and adversarial robustness for national security. Responses were due 22 June 2026 — a signal that DARPA is doubling down on the 'can we trust and verify it' side of AI.

latest.label: Cyber-AI proven and freed
latest.plain: At DEF CON in August 2025, Team Atlanta won the $4M top prize ahead of Trail of Bits and Theori. Across the final, the AI systems found dozens of planted flaws plus 18 real-world ones, and patched many automatically — then DARPA had all finalists open-source their systems for public use.

suppliers.name: Two Six Technologies
suppliers.role: GARD performer — built the 'Armory' adversarial-AI testing platform
suppliers.claim: "Armory was developed as part of the Guaranteeing AI Robustness against Deception (GARD) program under DARPA... to help engineers understand how models behave under adversarial conditions."

suppliers.name: IBM, MITRE, University of Chicago, Google Research
suppliers.role: GARD performers — adversarial-robustness libraries, datasets and open-source tools

suppliers.name: SRI International (and UCLA, CMU, UC Berkeley, Rockwell Collins, others)
suppliers.role: ANSR performers — neuro-symbolic AI algorithms and architectures

suppliers.name: Team Atlanta (Georgia Tech, Samsung Research, KAIST, POSTECH)
suppliers.role: AIxCC winner — top cyber-reasoning system, open-sourced
suppliers.claim: "As hackers we started this competition as AI skeptics, but now we truly believe in the potential of adopting large language models when solving security problems." — Prof. Taesoo Kim

suppliers.name: Lockheed Martin
suppliers.role: ACE / AI-tools contractor — modified the X-62A and supports DARPA AI work
suppliers.claim: "The DARPA AIR program will use state-of-the-art ML technology and Lockheed Martin's ARISE infrastructure to deliver unprecedented amounts of data that service members can use for faster decisions."
suppliers.sourceName: Lockheed Martin — DARPA AI tools contract

sources.name: DARPA — ACE world first in aerospace

unknowns: DARPA publishes results and named performers, but the full dollar value, classified follow-on work, and which capabilities ultimately reach fielded systems are often not public. Some performer lists (e.g. full ANSR and AIxCC rosters) are only partially disclosed, and the AI Forge's funded projects were still being assembled as of mid-2026.
